posts.title: Hello World
posts.text: Welcome to Hexo! This is your very first post. Check documentation for more info. If you get any problems when using Hexo, you can find the answer in troubleshooting or you can ask me on GitHub. Quick StartCreate a new post1$ hexo new "My New Post" More info: Writing Run server1$ hexo server More info: Server Generate static files1$ hexo generate More info: Generating Deploy to remote sites1$ hexo deploy More info: Deployment

posts.title: 3. From Statistical Mechanics to Thermodynamics
posts.text:
3.1 Liouville’s the theorem and equationThe total number of ensemble members is constant. So phase space volume must be equal to the rate at which the ensemble members leave through the surface S. This is exactly same conditon that we impose to derive the mass continuity equation. ex) Phase Space Equation ex) Fluid Dynamics Equation In statistical mechanics, we consider the phase space, which is a 6𝑁 dimensional space for a system with 𝑁 particles. Each point in this space is represented by: where: are the position coordinates. are the momentum coordinates. We can imagine as “velocity” in the phase space. be the ensemble density in phase spaceSo the continuity equation in phase space is .. where: is the divergence operator in the -dimensional phase space. represents the probability current in phase space. Divergence Operator in Phase Space is The divergence operator in phase space is defined as: [
abla*{\mathbf{Z}} = \left( rac{\partial}{\partial q_1}, \ldots,  rac{\partial}{\partial q*{3N}},  rac{\partial}{\partial p1}, \ldots,  rac{\partial}{\partial p{3N}}ight).] This operator takes into account all position and momentum coordinates in the (6N)-dimensional space. 5. Applying the Divergence TheoremTo derive the continuity equation in phase space, we use the divergence theorem, which states: [\int*{\Omega} 
abla*{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot (ho \dot{\mathbf{Z}}) , d\mathbf{Z} = \int_{S} (ho \dot{\mathbf{Z}}) \cdot d\mathbf{S},] where: (\Omega) is an arbitrary volume in phase space. (S) is the boundary surface of (\Omega). The left side represents the total divergence of the probability current inside (\Omega), and the right side represents the flux of this current across the boundary (S). For any arbitrary choice of (\Omega), the equality implies the local form of the continuity equation: [ rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} + 
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot (ho \dot{\mathbf{Z}}) = 0.] 6. ensemble densityWe can expand the divergence term (
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot (ho \dot{\mathbf{Z}})): [
abla*{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot (ho \dot{\mathbf{Z}}) = \dot{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot 
abla*{\mathbf{Z}} ho + ho 
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot \dot{\mathbf{Z}},] where: (\dot{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot 
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} ho) represents the advection of the ensemble density along the flow in phase space. (ho 
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot \dot{\mathbf{Z}}) represents the divergence of the phase space velocity. 7. Material Derivative and Liouville’s EquationWe can introduce the material derivative (or total derivative) to describe the change in (ho) as we move with the flow in phase space: [ rac{Dho}{Dt} \equiv  rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} + \dot{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot 
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} ho.] The continuity equation can now be rewritten using the material derivative: [ rac{Dho}{Dt} = -ho 
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot \dot{\mathbf{Z}}.] In a conservative system, where the phase space flow is incompressible ((
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot \dot{\mathbf{Z}} = 0)), this simplifies to: [ rac{Dho}{Dt} = 0 \implies  rac{\partial ho}{\partial t} + \dot{\mathbf{Z}} \cdot 
abla_{\mathbf{Z}} ho = 0.] This is known as Liouville’s equation, which states that the ensemble density is constant along the trajectories of the system in phase space. 8. Conclusion The continuity equation in phase space mirrors the mass continuity equation in fluid dynamics, expressing the conservation of probability density rather than mass. The velocity (\dot{\mathbf{Z}}) in phase space is analogous to the fluid velocity (\mathbf{u}). Liouville’s theorem implies that, for a conservative system, the density of microstates in phase space remains constant as the system evolves. This analogy provides a powerful way to understand the behavior of complex systems in statistical mechanics using concepts familiar from fluid dynamics.
